tool_calls.name: query_aas_graph

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(area:SubmodelElementCollection)
WHERE (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(p:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
WITH aas, area, p
OPTIONAL MATCH (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(areaNameProp:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, areaNameProp.value AS area_name
ORDER BY aas.idShort

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(area:SubmodelElementCollection)
WHERE (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
WITH aas, area
MATCH (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(areaNameProp:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, areaNameProp.value AS area_name
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(area:SubmodelElementCollection)
WHERE (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
WITH aas, area
MATCH (area)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(areaNameProp:Property)
WHERE areaNameProp.idShort = 'AreaName'
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, areaNameProp.value AS area_name
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(area:SubmodelElementCollection)
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, area.idShort AS area_idShort
LIMIT 10

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, sm.id AS sm_id, sm.idShort AS sm_idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id AS semanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, areaProp.value AS area_value

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
WHERE areaProp.value = 8000
WITH hallAas
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(deviceSm:Submodel)
WHERE deviceSm.idShort <> 'FacilityInformation'
MATCH (deviceSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(deviceElement:SubmodelElement)
RETURN hallAas.id AS hall_id, hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, deviceSm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, deviceElement.idShort AS element_idShort, labels(deviceElement) AS element_types
LIMIT 50

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
WHERE areaProp.value = 8000
WITH hallAas
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(deviceSm:Submodel)
RETURN hallAas.id AS hall_id, hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, deviceSm.id AS sm_id, deviceSm.idShort AS sm_idShort

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
WHERE areaProp.value = 8000
RETURN hallAas.id AS hall_id, hallAas.idShort AS hall_name

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
WHERE areaProp.value = 8000
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property {idShort: 'Area'})
WHERE areaProp.value = 8000
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, areaProp.value AS area_value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'Area' AND areaProp.value = 8000
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, areaProp.value AS area_value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'Area'
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, areaProp.value AS area_value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'Area' AND areaProp.value = 8000
WITH aas
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(deviceSm:Submodel)
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, deviceSm.id AS sm_id, deviceSm.idShort AS sm_idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, sm.id AS sm_id, sm.idShort AS sm_idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort <> 'FacilityInformation'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(deviceElement:SubmodelElement)
RETURN aas.id AS hall_id, aas.idShort AS hall_name, sm.idShort AS sm_idShort, deviceElement.idShort AS element_idShort, labels(deviceElement) AS element_types
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(deviceEntity:Entity)
WHERE deviceEntity.idShort IN ['UR20_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'MiR250_001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (deviceEntity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN deviceEntity.idShort AS device_name, asset.globalAssetId AS global_asset_id, asset.assetType AS asset_type

tool_calls.result_preview:
{
  "name": "Data Model for Asset Location",
  "version": "1.0.1",

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaNameProp:Property {idShort: 'AreaName'})
WHERE areaNameProp.value = 'Hall 3'
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.id AS submodelId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId
LIMIT 20

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id AS semanticId
ORDER BY sc.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

args.name: Hierarchical Structures enabling Bills of Material

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, aas.category
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN hall.idShort, sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN entity.idShort, entity.smId, entity.entityType
LIMIT 20

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entry:Entity {idShort: 'EntryNode'})
MATCH (entry)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(device:Entity)
WHERE device.idShort <> 'EntryNode'
RETURN device.idShort
ORDER BY device.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR20_001', 'UR3e_001']
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id
ORDER BY aas.idShort

result.query: Which devices are located in Hall 3?
result.response:
I need to find which devices (Asset Administration Shells) are located in Hall 3. This requires querying the graph for assets associated with Hall 3.

First, let me check the graph schema and templates to understand how location information is structured in this AAS environment.

















I can see there's a "Data Model for Asset Location" template with semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0` which is used for tracking asset locations. Let me query the graph to find assets located in Hall 3.

First, let me check what semanticIds are actually present in the graph related to location:









I need to use exact matches for semanticIds. Let me first list all distinct semanticIds in the graph to find the location-related ones:









I see the semanticIds in the graph. I notice there's a "FacilityInformation" template but I don't see the "Data Model for Asset Location" template (`https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`) in the graph. Let me check if there are any submodels with location information by looking at the FacilityInformation template structure and also check for any assets that might have location data.

Let me first get the template structure for FacilityInformation to understand how location data is stored:









The FacilityInformation template is for facility data (halls), not for asset locations. Let me check if there's a different way location information is stored. Let me look for the "Data Model for Asset Location" template structure:









Now I understand the AssetLocation template structure. It has `VisitedAreas` which contains `AreaName` that could include "Hall 3". However, I noticed that the "Data Model for Asset Location" template semanticId (`https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`) is NOT in the graphSemanticIds list from the templates index, meaning no submodels with this semanticId exist in the graph.

Let me check what submodels actually exist in the graph and see if there's any location information stored differently. Let me first list all AAS and their submodels:









I can see there's a "Hall3" AAS (`urn:aas:hall3`) with a `HierarchicalStructures` submodel. The HierarchicalStructures template is used for bills of material and can contain Entity nodes that represent assets. Let me check the HierarchicalStructures template to understand how it structures contained assets:









The HierarchicalStructures template uses Entity nodes with relationships like `HasPart`, `IsPartOf`, etc. Let me query the graph to find the Hall3 AAS and traverse its HierarchicalStructures submodel to find assets contained in Hall 3. I'll look for Entity nodes that have `REPRESENTS_ASSET` relationships:









I found the devices in Hall 3. Let me also get the AAS idShort values for these assets to provide more complete information:









Based on the graph data, I can now provide the answer. The devices located in Hall 3 are:

**According to the graph data**, the following devices are located in Hall 3:

1. **MiR250_001** (Mobile Industrial Robot)
2. **MiR250_002** (Mobile Industrial Robot)
3. **UR3e_001** (Universal Robots robot)
4. **UR20_001** (Universal Robots robot)

Note: "Hall3" itself appears as an entity in the hierarchical structure, but it is the facility container, not a device.

These assets are registered in the HierarchicalStructures submodel of the Hall3 Asset Administration Shell, which tracks the containment relationship between the hall and the devices located within it.

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem:SubmodelElement)
RETURN aas.idShort AS device_name, aas.id AS device_id, elem.idShort AS element_idShort, elem.value AS element_value
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'PrimaryUse'
RETURN aas.idShort AS device_name, aas.id AS device_id, areaProp.value AS primary_use

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(useProp:MultiLanguageProperty)
WHERE useProp.idShort = 'PrimaryUse'
OPTIONAL MATCH (useProp)-[:HAS_VALUE]->(langString:LangString)
RETURN aas.idShort AS device_name, aas.id AS device_id, langString.text AS primary_use_text

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'Area'
RETURN aas.idShort AS device_name, aas.id AS device_id, areaProp.value AS area_value

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id, sc.id AS template_semanticId

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN entity.idShort AS entity_name, entity.entityType AS entity_type

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN entity.idShort AS entity_name, entity.entityType AS entity_type

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
RETURN sm.id AS submodel_id

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
RETURN sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN entity.idShort AS entity_name, entity.entityType AS entity_type, entity.smId AS entity_smId

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(child:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (child)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN parent.idShort AS parent_name, child.idShort AS child_name, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id

I will search the graph for assets that are autonomous transport robots and identify their locations.

2.  **Query the graph:** Find all `AssetAdministrationShell` nodes that manage an `Asset` of type "robot" or have a submodel matching the transport robot template.
3.  **Extract location:** Look for location properties within the asset data or associated submodels (e.g., "Location", "Position", "Site").
4.  **Resolve semantics:** If location data is found via semantic IDs, resolve them to human-readable names.

Let's start by checking the templates index for autonomous transport robots.









I found a template specifically for "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" (AGV) which are autonomous transport robots. The semantic ID is `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0`.

Let me check if there are instance shells derived from these types, and also look for any other submodels that might contain location data. I'll also check the structure of the "Data Model for Asset Location" template to ensure I'm querying the correct element path.









The "Data Model for Asset Location" template is quite complex. It doesn't have a simple "Location" property. Instead, it has:
- `Addresses` (with street, city, etc.)
- `CoordinateSystems` (with X, Y coordinates)
- `VisitedAreas` (with AreaName, AreaId)
- `AssetTraces` (with LocationRecords containing Position X, Y, Z)

The location might be stored in `VisitedAreas` -> `AreaName` or `Addresses`. Let me also check if there are instance shells (not just types) and look for any simpler location properties.

First, let me find all instance shells derived from the type shells I found, and also check if there are any other AAS shells that might be instances.









I found 3 instance shells:

3. MiR250_001 (derived from MiR250_Type)

Now let me check if these instance shells have the "Data Model for Asset Location" submodel and extract location information from it. I'll look for `VisitedAreas` -> `AreaName` or `Addresses` -> `Street`/`Citytown`.









No location data found in the "Data Model for Asset Location" submodel for these instances. Let me check what submodels these instance shells actually have, and also look for any other location-related properties that might be in the Technical Data AGV submodel or elsewhere.

I don't see the "Technical Data AGV" submodel or "Asset Location" submodel in the instance shells. Let me check if the Technical Data AGV submodel exists on the instance shells by looking specifically for it.









The instance shells don't have the "Technical Data AGV" submodel. Let me check what submodels the type shells have, and also look for any location-related data in the Asset nodes or other properties.

Let me also search for any properties that might contain location information in the graph, regardless of the submodel template.

The asset_type is null, so that doesn't help with location. Let me check if there are any other submodels in the graph that might contain location information, such as "FacilityInformation" which I saw in the templates index.









I found two facilities: Hall3 and Hall4. These might be the locations where the robots are situated. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robots and these halls, or if the robots have any reference to these facilities.

Let me also check the FacilityInformation submodel to see what location data it contains, and if there's any way to link the robots to these halls.









The FacilityInformation template has `Area`, `PrimaryUse`, and `PowerConnection` properties. Let me check what values these halls have, and also look for any relationship between the robots and these halls.









I found that Hall3 has an area of 8000 and Hall4 has an area of 12000. The primary_use is null (possibly stored in a different way for MultiLanguageProperty).

Let me search for any submodel that might contain location information by looking at all submodels across all AAS and their semantic IDs.









Now I can see the full picture. The robots (MiR100_001, MiR250_001, MiR250_002) only have `Nameplate` and `ServiceRequestNotification` submodels. The `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel is only on the Type shells (MiR100_Type, MiR250_Type), not on the instance shells.

The halls (Hall3, Hall4) have `FacilityInformation` and `HierarchicalStructures` submodels.

args.cypher:
MATCH (typeAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(typeAas)
OPTIONAL MATCH (instanceAas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN typeAas.idShort AS type_name, instanceAas.idShort AS instance_name, instanceAas.id AS instance_id, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id

MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
MATCH (instanceAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)
WHERE (locSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'AreaName'
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(addrProp:Property)
WHERE addrProp.idShort = 'Citytown' OR addrProp.idShort = 'Street'
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, 
       areaProp.value AS area_name,
       addrProp.value AS address_detail,
       addrProp.idShort AS address_type

MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
MATCH (instanceAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
MATCH (instanceAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, sm.id AS submodel_id

tool_calls.name: query_aas_graph

args.cypher:
MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
MATCH (instanceAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
WHERE prop.idShort CONTAINS 'Location' OR prop.idShort CONTAINS 'Place' OR prop.idShort CONTAINS 'Site' OR prop.idShort CONTAINS 'Area'
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, prop.idShort AS property_name, prop.value AS value

tool_calls.result_preview: {"error":"forbidden_pattern","violations":[{"rule":"idShort_contains_or_regex","hint":"Use exact match instead: WHERE aas.idShort = 'ExactName'. See cypher.md anti-pattern #4.","match":"prop.idShort C

args.cypher:
MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
MATCH (instanceAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(prop:Property)
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, prop.idShort AS property_name, prop.value AS value
LIMIT 50

MATCH (instanceAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE instanceAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (instanceAas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN instanceAas.idShort AS robot_name, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id, asset.assetKind AS asset_kind, asset.assetType AS asset_type

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS facility_name, aas.id AS facility_id, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(areaProp:Property)
WHERE areaProp.idShort = 'Area'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(useProp:MultiLanguageProperty)
WHERE useProp.idShort = 'PrimaryUse'
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, areaProp.value AS area, useProp.text AS primary_use

MATCH (robotAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE robotAas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (robotAas)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(typeAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
OPTIONAL MATCH (robotAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(ref:ReferenceElement)
RETURN robotAas.idShort AS robot_name, typeAas.idShort AS type_name, ref.idShort AS ref_idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN DISTINCT aas.idShort AS aas_name, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sc.id AS semantic_id
ORDER BY aas_name, submodel_idShort

args.name: Hierarchical Structures enabling Bills of Material

args.cypher:
MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE hallAas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(hsSm:Submodel)
WHERE (hsSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
MATCH (hsSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, entity.idShort AS entity_idShort, labels(entity) AS entity_labels

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE hallAas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(hsSm:Submodel)
WHERE (hsSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, hsSm.id AS submodel_id

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE hallAas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(hsSm:Submodel)
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, hsSm.idShort AS submodel_idShort

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE hallAas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(hsSm:Submodel)
WHERE hsSm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (hsSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, entity.idShort AS entity_idShort, entity.entityType AS entity_type

MATCH (hallAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE hallAas.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (hallAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(hsSm:Submodel)
WHERE hsSm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (hsSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(rel:RelationshipElement)
WHERE rel.idShort = 'IsPartOf'
MATCH (rel)-[:HAS_FIRST]->(child:Entity)
MATCH (rel)-[:HAS_SECOND]->(parent:Entity)
RETURN hallAas.idShort AS hall_name, child.idShort AS child_asset, parent.idShort AS parent_container

result.response:
I need to find autonomous transport robots in the AAS environment and their locations. Let me start by getting the graph schema and templates index to understand the structure, then query for robots.









Now let me get the templates index to identify templates related to autonomous transport robots or mobile robots.









I can see there's a template for "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" (AGV) which is relevant for autonomous transport robots. Let me also search for any submodels that might identify robots or mobile robots. First, let me query the graph to find all AAS and their submodels to identify which ones are autonomous transport robots.

I'll look for:
1. Submodels with the AGV technical data semanticId
2. Any CapabilityDescription submodels that might indicate robot capabilities
3. Assets that might be robots

Let me start by querying for all AAS with their submodels and semanticIds to identify robots:









I can see from the results that there are several AAS instances. I notice:
- **MiR100_001** - This appears to be a mobile robot (MiR = Mobile Industrial Robot)
- **MiR250_001** and **MiR250_002** - More mobile robots
- The type shells (MiR100_Type, MiR250_Type) have **TechnicalDataAGV** submodels with semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0` which is for "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles"

The MiR robots are autonomous transport robots (AGVs/AMRs). Now I need to find their locations. Let me check if there's location information in the graph. I should look for:
1. AssetLocation submodels
2. HierarchicalStructures that might show where these robots are located
3. Any properties that indicate location

Let me query for the AssetLocation template and check if any robots have location data:









I need to use exact matches. Let me query for each robot individually or list all AAS and filter manually. Let me first get all AAS with their submodels to identify the MiR robots:









I can see the robots:
- **MiR100_001** (urn:aas:mir100:001)
- **MiR250_001** (urn:aas:mir250:001)
- **MiR250_002** (urn:aas:mir250:002)

Now I need to find their locations. Let me check if there's location information in the HierarchicalStructures of the halls (Hall3, Hall4) or if there's an AssetLocation submodel. Let me also check the type shells to see what submodels they have that might indicate location tracking:









Good, I can see the type shells have TechnicalDataAGV submodels. Now let me check if there's location information by looking at the HierarchicalStructures in the halls to see if they contain references to the robots, or check if there's an AssetLocation submodel:









Let me check the HierarchicalStructures to see if they contain references to the robots (which would indicate their location):









The HierarchicalStructures don't seem to contain Entity nodes with REPRESENTS_ASSET links. Let me check if there's an AssetLocation submodel anywhere, or look for location properties in the robots themselves. Let me also check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel to see if it contains location information:









The TechnicalDataAGV template contains technical specifications but not real-time location data. Let me check if there's an AssetLocation submodel that tracks the current location of assets. Let me also look for any location-related properties in the robots or check if the HierarchicalStructures contain references to the robots:









The query didn't find any AssetLocation submodels. Let me check the HierarchicalStructures more carefully to see if they contain references to the robots, which would indicate their location:









Let me fix the Cypher syntax:









The HierarchicalStructures don't seem to have any elements. Let me check if there's location information stored elsewhere. Let me look for any properties related to location in the robots or check if there's a specific submodel that tracks robot locations. Let me also check the CapabilityDescription submodels to see if they mention location:









The instance robots don't have CapabilityDescription submodels - only the type shells do. Let me check if there's any location information in the Nameplate or other submodels of the robots. Let me also search for any location-related properties:









I can see the robot has properties like SerialNumber, DateOfManufacture, etc., but no location information. Let me check if there's an AssetLocation submodel that might track the current location of assets. Let me also check the FacilityInformation submodels in the halls to see if they contain information about which robots are in which hall:









The search results are from UR robot documentation, not MiR robots. Let me search specifically for MiR robot documentation to see if there's location information:









The TechnicalDataAGV submodel doesn't have indexed documents. Let me check if there's any location information by looking at the FacilityInformation submodels in the halls to see what they contain:









The FacilityInformation submodels contain area and power connection data but don't list which robots are in which hall.

Based on my investigation, I can identify the autonomous transport robots in the system, but there doesn't appear to be real-time location tracking data in the AAS graph. Let me summarize what I found:

**Autonomous Transport Robots Identified:**

1. **MiR100_001** (AAS ID: `urn:aas:mir100:001`)
   - Type: MiR100 (Mobile Industrial Robot 100kg capacity)
   - Has TechnicalDataAGV submodel in its type shell

2. **MiR250_001** (AAS ID: `urn:aas:mir250:001`)
   - Type: MiR250 (Mobile Industrial Robot 250kg capacity)
   - Has TechnicalDataAGV submodel in its type shell

3. **MiR250_002** (AAS ID: `urn:aas:mir250:002`)
   - Type: MiR250 (Mobile Industrial Robot 250kg capacity)
   - Has TechnicalDataAGV submodel in its type shell

**Location Information:**
The AAS graph does not currently contain real-time location tracking data for these robots. There is:
- No AssetLocation submodel present in the system
- No HierarchicalStructures that reference the robots to indicate their location
- No location properties in the robot submodels

The system has two halls (Hall3 and Hall4) with FacilityInformation submodels, but these only contain area and power connection data, not robot assignments.

**Conclusion:**
According to the graph data, we have **3 autonomous transport robots** (1x MiR100 and 2x MiR250), but their current locations are not tracked in the AAS system. The robots are Mobile Industrial Robots (MiR) which are AGVs/AMRs used for autonomous material transport in intralogistics.

The "Data Model for Asset Location" submodel (semanticId: `https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`) is **not present** on any of these robot instances. The robots currently only have:

To get real-time or last-known locations, the Asset Location submodel would need to be added to each robot's AAS, or location data would need to be queried from an external tracking system. According to the graph data, no location tracking is currently configured for these assets.

Based on the graph data, I can identify the autonomous transport robots (AGVs) in the system. The robots are identified by the "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" submodel template.

**Autonomous Transport Robots Found:**

1. **MiR100_001** (ID: `urn:aas:mir100:001`)

   - Global Asset ID: `urn:asset:mir100:001`

2. **MiR250_001** (ID: `urn:aas:mir250:001`)
   - Type: MiR250
   - Global Asset ID: `urn:asset:mir250:001`

3. **MiR250_002** (ID: `urn:aas:mir250:002`)
   - Type: MiR250
   - Global Asset ID: `urn:asset:mir250:002`

The current graph data does not contain location information for these robots. The "Data Model for Asset Location" submodel template (`https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`) is available in the templates index but is not currently implemented in any of the robot shells. Each robot currently only has:

- Multiple "ServiceRequestNotification" submodels (for maintenance/service requests)

To get location data, the Asset Location submodel would need to be added to each robot's AAS with properties like "Location", "Hall", "Zone", or "CurrentLocation".

**Summary:**
- **3 autonomous transport robots** are registered in the system
- **1 MiR100** and **2 MiR250** models
- **No location data** is currently available in the AAS graph
- The robots are identified by their Asset Administration Shells and have technical data conforming to the IDTA "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template

result.response:
I will find all autonomous transport robots in the system and their locations.

**Expected structure:** A list of robot assets (likely with names or IDs) and their associated location information (e.g., hall, zone, or specific coordinate data stored in submodels).

**Plan:**
1.  Retrieve the templates index to identify the semantic ID for "Location" or "Capability" templates that might define robot types.
2.  Query the graph to find all `AssetAdministrationShell` nodes that manage an `Asset` of type "Robot" or have a submodel indicating "Autonomous Transport".
3.  For each found robot, traverse to find location-related submodels or properties.

Let's start by getting the templates index to understand how robots and locations are modeled.









I see a relevant template: **"Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles"** (`TechnicalDataAGV`) with semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0`. This likely describes the robots.
I also see **"Data Model for Asset Location"** (`AssetLocation`) with semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0` for location data.

Now I will query the graph to find all submodels conforming to the AGV template, then link them to their AAS and assets, and finally look for location data.









I found two robot types: `MiR250_Type` and `MiR100_Type`. However, these appear to be **Type** shells (indicated by `_Type` in the name and the `asset_id` ending in `:type`), not instance shells. The location data is likely on the **instance** shells derived from these types.

I need to find the instance shells derived from these types and check for location data on them.









I need to avoid `CONTAINS`. I will use the exact semanticId match for the AGV submodel instead of checking `idShort`.









I found three robot instances: `MiR100_001`, `MiR250_002`, and `MiR250_001`. However, none of them currently have a location submodel linked directly via the `AssetLocation` semanticId.

Let me check if location information is stored differently, perhaps as a property within the `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel or another submodel on these instance shells. I'll query for all submodels on these instance shells to see what's available.









The result is dominated by `ServiceRequestNotification` submodels (likely many instances). I need to filter for the specific submodels that might contain location data. Let me look for the `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel on these instances and also check for any submodel that might contain location properties (like `CurrentLocation` or similar).

Let me first check the structure of the `TechnicalDataAGV` template to see if it contains location fields.









The `TechnicalDataAGV` template contains technical specifications but **no current location data**. Location data would typically be in a separate `AssetLocation` submodel or a real-time tracking submodel.

Let me check if there are any other submodels on the instance shells that might contain location information, perhaps by looking for properties with "Location" in their `idShort` or semanticId.









I cannot use `CONTAINS` on `idShort`. Let me try a different approach: list all submodels on the instance shells and check their semanticIds to see if any match location-related templates, or list all properties and look for location-related ones by their semanticId.

First, let me check what submodels exist on these instances and their semanticIds:









I see the submodels on these instances are mostly `Nameplate` and many `ServiceRequestNotification` entries. There is no `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel visible in this result, and no `AssetLocation` submodel.

Let me check if the `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel exists on these instances by looking for it specifically:

result.response:
I need to find autonomous transport robots in the AAS environment and determine their locations. Let me start by checking the available templates and then querying the graph for robots.









I can see there's a "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template which is relevant for autonomous transport robots (AGVs). Let me now query the graph to find all Asset Administration Shells that have submodels related to AGVs or autonomous robots, and also check for location information.









I need to use exact matches for semanticIds. Let me first get the exact semanticId from the templates index for the AGV template, then query using exact match.









I found two type shells for AGVs (MiR250 and MiR100). Now I need to find the instance shells that are derived from these types, and also look for location information. Let me also check if there are any instance shells directly.









I found 3 instance robots: MiR250_001, MiR250_002, and MiR100_001. Now I need to find their locations. Let me check for the "Data Model for Asset Location" template which should contain location information.









The AssetLocation template doesn't seem to be used directly. Let me check what submodels the instance robots have and look for location-related elements within them.









I see the robots have Nameplate submodels and many ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check the Nameplate submodel for location information, and also look for any FacilityInformation or location-related data. Let me also check if there are any Facility shells that might contain location information.









I found two halls (Hall3 and Hall4) with FacilityInformation. Now let me check if the robots are associated with these halls. Let me look for relationships between the robots and halls, or check if there's location information in the robot submodels.









The Nameplate submodels contain serial numbers and manufacturing info but not location. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robots and the halls, or if location information is stored elsewhere. Let me also check the FacilityInformation submodels to see what location data they contain.









The FacilityInformation submodels contain area, primary use, and power connection info for the halls, but I need to find if there's a relationship between the robots and the halls. Let me check if there are any relationships or if the robots are managed by the halls.









Let me check if there are any relationships between the robots and halls, or if the robots are located in a specific hall. Let me also check the Asset nodes to see if there's location information there.









Let me check if there's a hierarchical relationship between halls and robots, or if there's any container structure. Let me also search for any location-related properties in the robot submodels.









I need to avoid using CONTAINS. Let me list all properties in the robot submodels to see if there's any location information.









I don't see explicit location properties in the robot submodels. Let me check if there's a hierarchical container structure where halls contain robots, or if there's a different way location is tracked. Let me also check if there are any Entity nodes that might represent location relationships.









Excellent! I found the location information! The halls contain Entity nodes that represent the robots located in them. Let me verify this by checking the REPRESENTS_ASSET relationships to confirm which robots are in which halls.









I now have all the information needed. Let me summarize:

**Autonomous Transport Robots Found:**
1. **MiR250_001** - Located in **Hall3**
2. **MiR250_002** - Located in **Hall3**
3. **MiR100_001** - Located in **Hall4**

These are MiR (Mobile Industrial Robots) - specifically MiR250 and MiR100 models, which are autonomous mobile robots used for transport in intralogistics.

According to the graph data:
- **Hall3** contains two MiR250 robots (MiR250_001 and MiR250_002)
- **Hall4** contains one MiR100 robot (MiR100_001)

The robots are identified as autonomous transport robots through their association with the "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" (TechnicalDataAGV) submodel template, which is specifically designed for AGVs in intralogistics.

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
AND sm.idShort = 'Nameplate'
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
WHERE p.idShort = 'UniqueFacilityIdentifier'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot, p.value AS facility_id

records.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?

result.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE locSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(locProp:Property)
WHERE locProp.idShort = 'Location' OR locProp.idShort = 'CurrentLocation' OR locProp.idShort = 'Position'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, aas.id AS aas_id, asset.globalAssetId, sm.id AS agv_submodel_id, locProp.value AS location

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(typeAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
MATCH (typeAas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(typeLocSm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(typeLocSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE typeLocSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
OPTIONAL MATCH (typeLocSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(locProp:Property)
WHERE locProp.idShort = 'Location' OR locProp.idShort = 'CurrentLocation' OR locProp.idShort = 'Position'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, aas.id AS aas_id, typeLocSm.id AS location_submodel_id, locProp.value AS location

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE locSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(locProp:Property)
WHERE locProp.idShort = 'Location' OR locProp.idShort = 'CurrentLocation' OR locProp.idShort = 'Position' OR locProp.idShort = 'Hall' OR locProp.idShort = 'Area'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, aas.id AS aas_id, locSm.id AS location_submodel_id, locProp.idShort AS loc_field, locProp.value AS location

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, aas.id AS aas_id, locSm.idShort AS location_submodel_idShort, locSm.id AS location_submodel_id, locSc.id AS location_semanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)

MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)

MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(locProp:Property)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, locProp.idShort AS loc_field, locProp.value AS location

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE locSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0' OR locSm.idShort = 'AssetLocation'
MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(locProp:Property)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, locProp.idShort AS loc_field, locProp.value AS location

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE locSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem)
WHERE elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Location' OR elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Position' OR elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Hall' OR elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Area' OR elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Zone' OR elem.idShort CONTAINS 'Facility'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, elem.idShort AS element_idShort, elem.value AS value

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(locSm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(locSc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE locSc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
MATCH (locSm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem)
WITH aas.idShort AS robot_name, elem
WHERE elem.idShort IN ['Location', 'CurrentLocation', 'Position', 'Hall', 'Area', 'Zone', 'Facility', 'LocationName', 'CurrentPosition', 'FacilityName']
RETURN robot_name, elem.idShort AS element_idShort, elem.value AS value

records.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?

result.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?
result.response:
I need to find autonomous transport robots in the AAS graph and determine their locations. Let me start by getting the necessary context and then query for robots.









I can see there's a "Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles" template which is relevant for autonomous transport robots. Let me also check for location-related templates. I see "Data Model for Asset Location" which could help with location information.

Now let me query the graph to find all Asset Administration Shells that might be autonomous transport robots. I'll look for shells with submodels related to AGV (Automated Guided Vehicles) or capability descriptions that indicate they are transport robots.









I found two type shells for MiR robots (MiR250 and MiR100). Now I need to find the instance shells that are derived from these types, as those would be the actual robots deployed on the factory floor. Let me also check if there are any instance shells directly.









I found 3 instance robots:
- MiR100_001 (derived from MiR100_Type)
- MiR250_002 (derived from MiR250_Type)
- MiR250_001 (derived from MiR250_Type)

Now I need to find their locations. The "Data Model for Asset Location" template has semanticId `https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0`. Let me check if any of these robots have location submodels, or if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data.

Let me first check what submodels each instance shell has:









The query returned many ServiceRequestNotification entries which are not helpful for location. Let me look for location information differently. I should check if there's a FacilityInformation submodel that might contain location data, or look for AssetLocation submodels. Let me also check what submodels exist in the graph that might contain location information.









I found FacilityInformation submodels for Hall4 and Hall3. These likely contain location information. Let me check which AAS shells these belong to and what properties they contain.









So we have Hall4 and Hall3 as facility shells. Now I need to find out which robots are located in which hall. Let me check if there's a relationship between the robots and the halls, or if the location information is stored in the robots' submodels.

Let me look for any submodels on the robot instances that might contain location data, or check if there's a CapabilityDescription that might indicate location:









The robots only have Nameplate and ServiceRequestNotification submodels. Let me check if there's a different way to find robot locations. Perhaps the robots are associated with halls through a different mechanism. Let me look for any Entity nodes or relationships that might connect robots to locations.

Let me also check the FacilityInformation submodels to see what properties they contain - they might list the robots in each hall:









The FacilityInformation submodel contains Area and PowerConnection properties, but not robot listings. Let me check Hall3 as well, and then look for a different approach to find robot locations.









The FacilityInformation submodels don't contain robot location data directly. Let me check if there's a CapabilityDescription submodel that might indicate where robots are deployed, or look for any other submodels that might contain location information.

Let me also check if there are any other AAS shells in the graph that might be related to robot locations:









I see there are Hall3 and Hall4 shells, and robot shells (MiR100, MiR250). Let me check if there's a relationship between the robot shells and the hall shells. Perhaps the robots are managed by or related to the halls.

Let me also check if there's a CapabilityDescription submodel on any of the shells that might indicate location or deployment:









The CapabilityDescription is on the type shells, not the instance shells. Let me look at the CapabilityDescription template to understand what information it contains, and then check if there's location information there.









The CapabilityDescription template is complex and doesn't seem to directly contain location information. Let me try a different approach - perhaps the location information is stored in the TechnicalDataAGV submodel or in the robot's asset information.

Let me check the TechnicalDataAGV submodel on one of the type shells to see what properties it contains:









The TechnicalDataAGV contains technical parameters but not location information. Let me check if there's any relationship between the robot instances and the halls through the Asset nodes or other relationships.

Let me also check if there's a way to find the location of robots by looking at the Asset nodes managed by the AAS shells:









The Asset nodes don't have location information either. Let me check if there's a specific asset location submodel or if the location is stored somewhere else. Let me also look for any submodels that might contain location data by searching for "location" in the idShort or semanticId.

Let me try a different approach - perhaps the robots are associated with halls through a container relationship or some other mechanism. Let me check if there are any Entity nodes that might represent the robots in a hall context:









I found the key information! In the HierarchicalStructures submodels of Hall3 and Hall4, there are Entity nodes that represent the robots located in each hall. Let me extract the relevant information:

From Hall3's HierarchicalStructures:
- MiR250_002
- MiR250_001

From Hall4's HierarchicalStructures:
- MiR100_001

Let me query this more specifically to get a clean view of which robots are in which hall:









Let me check the exact semanticId in the graph for HierarchicalStructures:









The semanticId is correct. Let me try a different approach - query the HierarchicalStructures submodels directly:









I now have the complete information about the autonomous transport robots and their locations.

Based on the graph data, here are the autonomous transport robots (MiR - Mobile Industrial Robots) and their locations:

**Autonomous Transport Robots:**

1. **MiR100_001** - Located in **Hall4**
   - Type: MiR100 (derived from MiR100_Type)

2. **MiR250_001** - Located in **Hall3**
   - Type: MiR250 (derived from MiR250_Type)

3. **MiR250_002** - Located in **Hall3**
   - Type: MiR250 (derived from MiR250_Type)

**Summary:**
- **Total robots:** 3 autonomous transport robots
- **MiR100:** 1 unit (in Hall4)
- **MiR250:** 2 units (both in Hall3)

The robots are organized hierarchically in the Facility shells (Hall3 and Hall4) through the HierarchicalStructures submodel, which tracks which assets are located in each hall.

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.idShort IN ['MiR250_Type', 'MiR100_Type']
RETURN instance.id AS instance_id, instance.idShort AS instance_idShort, type.idShort AS type_idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sc.id AS semantic_id
ORDER BY aas.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id IN ['https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0', 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0']
RETURN sm.id AS submodel_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.id IN ['urn:submodel:hall4:facilityinformation', 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation']
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001']
AND NOT sm.idShort IN ['ServiceRequestNotification', 'Nameplate']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id, sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall4:facilityinformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem)
RETURN elem.idShort AS element_idShort, labels(elem) AS element_types, elem.value AS value

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem)
RETURN elem.idShort AS element_idShort, labels(elem) AS element_types, elem.value AS value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
RETURN aas.id AS aas_id, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/CapabilityDescription/1/0'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
RETURN aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:mir250:type:technicaldataagv'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(elem)
RETURN elem.idShort AS element_idShort, labels(elem) AS element_types, elem.value AS value
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(a:Asset)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:hall3', 'urn:aas:hall4']
RETURN aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, a.globalAssetId AS global_asset_id, a.assetKind AS asset_kind, a.assetType AS asset_type

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
RETURN aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, e.idShort AS entity_idShort, e.entityType AS entity_type

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(robot:Entity)
WHERE robot.idShort IN ['MiR100_001', 'MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002']
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall_name, robot.idShort AS robot_id
ORDER BY hall_name, robot_id

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(robot:Entity)
WHERE robot.idShort STARTS WITH 'MiR'
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall_name, robot.idShort AS robot_id
ORDER BY hall_name, robot_id

Now I need to find the load capacity property in the TechnicalDataAGV submodel. Let me first get the template structure to understand what elements exist:









I found the relevant property: **`MaxLoadMass`** (idShort) under `TechnicalParameters` in the `TechnicalDataAGV` submodel. This property has `valueType: xs:float` and semanticId `0173-1#02-ABJ258#001`.

- **MiR100_Type**: MaxLoadMass = 100 kg

The instances derive from these types. In AAS, instances typically inherit or reference the type's specifications. The question asks which transport robot can carry **more than 200 kg**.

- MiR100 (100 kg capacity) < 200 kg ✗

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I need to find the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) for the MiR250 robot first, then search its documentation for emergency stop information.

Let me start by querying the graph to find the MiR250 asset.









I need to follow the anti-patterns. Let me first get the manual page on Cypher patterns and then try an exact match approach.









Now I understand. I cannot use CONTAINS on idShort. Let me first list all AAS shells to see what's available, then identify the MiR250.









I found MiR250 assets. There are two instances (MiR250_001 and MiR250_002) and a type shell (MiR250_Type). Documentation typically lives on the Type AAS. Let me use the type shell to search for emergency stop information.

First, let me get the submodels of the MiR250_Type to find where documentation might be stored.









I see there's a "HandoverDocumentation" submodel which likely contains the operator manual. Let me search for emergency stop information in the documents associated with this submodel.









I found comprehensive information about emergency stops in the MiR250 operator manual. Let me summarize the key points from the documentation:

## Emergency Stop Information for MiR250

According to the operator manual (found in the HandoverDocumentation submodel):

### What is Emergency Stop?
Emergency stop is a state the robot enters when an Emergency stop button has been physically pressed. When in Emergency stop:
- The status lights turn **red**
- You cannot move the robot or send it on missions
- Internal safety contactors are switched, cutting power to the top application and all moving parts
- You can hear audible clicks when the safety contactors switch

### How to Exit Emergency Stop
1. **Release** the Emergency stop button
2. Press the **Resume** button (which will be flashing blue after releasing the E-stop)
3. The robot immediately resumes operating state

### Emergency Stop Features

**Shared Emergency Stop Circuit:**
- The robot has a shared circuit between the robot and top module
- Both can trigger each other into Emergency stop
- The MiR250 has one built-in Emergency stop button
- Additional Emergency stop buttons can be connected through the electrical interface

**Emergency Stop Circuit:**
- Goes through the Auxiliary emergency stop interface
- Uses a 24V signal that passes through all Emergency stop buttons
- When any button is pressed, the circuit breaks (0V signal), triggering Emergency stop
- The circuit uses two input pins and two output pins for redundancy

**Safety Interfaces:**
- **Safety A interface**: Has "Shared emergency stop in 1" (pin 3) and "Shared emergency stop out 1" (pin 6)
- **Safety B interface**: Has "Shared emergency stop in 2" (pin 3) and "Shared E-Stop out 2" (pin 6)

### Related Safety Stops
The manual also distinguishes between:
- **Emergency stop**: Triggered by pressing E-stop buttons (category 0 stop)
- **Protective stop**: Automatically triggered by safety functions (e.g., laser scanner detecting objects)

For detailed diagrams and pin configurations, see:
- [Page 84](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=84) - Emergency stop overview
- [Page 86](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86) - Emergency stop circuit details
- [Page 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205) - Safety interface pin descriptions

result.response:
I need to find the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) for the MiR 250 robot first, then search its documentation for emergency stop information.

Let me start by querying the graph to find the MiR 250 asset and its associated submodels containing documentation.









I need to use exact matching for idShort. Let me first get the templates index and then try a different approach to find the MiR 250.









Now let me query for all AAS shells to find the MiR 250. I'll list them first to identify the correct one.









I found the MiR 250 assets. There are two instances (MiR250_001 and MiR250_002) and a type shell (MiR250_Type). Documentation is typically on the type shell. Let me check the submodels of the type shell to find where the PDF documentation is stored.









I need to find which submodel contains the PDF documentation. Let me check for File elements in these submodels, particularly looking for PDF files.









Found it. The MiR 250 PDF documentation is in the HandoverDocumentation submodel. Now let me search the PDF documentation for information about emergency stops.









I found relevant information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. Let me compile the key findings:

According to the MiR 250 operator manual (PDF documentation), here's what it says about emergency stops:

**Key Points:**

1. **Usage Warning** (page 85):
   - "Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."

2. **Types of Stop** (page 83):
   - There are four different stopped states:
     - Operational stop
     - Protective stop
     - **Emergency stop**
     - Manual stop
   - The last three types of stop (including emergency stop) are monitored by the safety PLC.

3. **Hardware Error Troubleshooting** (page 209):
   - When troubleshooting hardware errors, one of the first checks is: "Check that the Emergency stop button is released."

4. **Safety Interface** (page 205):
   - The auxiliary safety function interface includes:
     - **Shared emergency stop in** input: When inactive, the robot goes into emergency stop
     - **Shared emergency stop out** output: Becomes inactive when the robot is in emergency stop

**Summary:**
The emergency stop is a safety-monitored state controlled by the safety PLC. The manual emphasizes that emergency stop buttons should only be used in actual emergency situations, not for routine stopping. When the emergency stop is activated (button pressed or input inactive), the robot enters an emergency stop state, which can be verified through the safety interface pins.

You can view the relevant pages directly:

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I need to find the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) for the MiR250 robot and then search its documentation for emergency stop information.

Let me start by getting the necessary schema and templates information, then search for the MiR250 asset.









Now let me search for the MiR250 asset in the graph:









I need to use a different approach. Let me first list all AAS shells to see what's available:









I found the MiR250 assets. There are:
- `MiR250_Type` (type shell: `urn:aas:mir250:type`)
- `MiR250_001` (instance: `urn:aas:mir250:001`)
- `MiR250_002` (instance: `urn:aas:mir250:002`)

For documentation, I should check the type shell first since documentation typically lives on the Type AAS. Let me find the submodels of the MiR250 type shell:









Now I need to find which submodel contains the PDF documentation. Let me check for File elements in these submodels:









I found the PDF document. The HandoverDocumentation submodel contains the MiR250 PDF file. Now let me search the document for emergency stop information:









I found relevant information about emergency stops in the MiR250 manual. Let me search for more specific details about emergency stop buttons and their operation:









Based on the search results from the MiR250 operator manual, here's what it says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information from MiR250 Manual

### Key Points:

**1. Usage Warning (Page 85)**
- **NOTICE**: Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

**2. Emergency Stop Circuit (Page 86)**
- The Emergency stop circuit goes through the Auxiliary emergency stop interface and connects to the top module
- Any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit
- When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop

**3. Shared Emergency Stop Function (Page 87)**
- This function consists of a shared circuit between the robot and top module
- Enables them to trigger each other into an Emergency stop

**4. Emergency Stop States (Page 98)**
The manual describes three cases:
1. Robot is not in Emergency stop → output is 24 V
2. Robot is in Emergency stop because it receives 0V input from the Shared emergency stop interface
3. Robot is in Emergency stop because the inputs are unequal

**5. Auxiliary Safety Interfaces (Page 205)**
- **Safety A** (Pin 3): Shared emergency stop in 1 - Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Safety A** (Pin 6): Shared emergency stop out 1 - Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop
- **Safety B** (Pin 3): Shared emergency stop in 2 - Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Safety B** (Pin 6): Shared E-Stop out 2 - Active when the robot is in emergency stop

**6. Color Indication (Page 18)**
- Blinking blue: The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)

### Summary:
The MiR250 has a shared emergency stop circuit that can be connected to external emergency stop buttons. When the circuit is broken (0V or unequal inputs), the robot enters emergency stop mode. The robot can be connected to external safety systems through the Auxiliary safety function interfaces (Safety A and Safety B), which support shared emergency stop inputs and outputs.

For the complete details, you can view the manual at: [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

result.response:
According to the MiR250 operator manual (Handover Documentation submodel), here is what it says about emergency stops:

**Key Safety Notice** (page 85):
> "Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."

**Common Issues & Diagnostics** (page 99):
> "When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**."

**Maintenance Checks** (page 178):
> "In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, see if Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover is Clean."

**Status Indication** (page 18):
> "Blinking blue: The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)."

Let me search for more specific information about the emergency stop button location and reset procedure.









Based on the search results from the MiR 250 operator manual, here is what the documentation says about emergency stops:

## Emergency Stop Information from MiR 250 Manual

### Key Points:

1. **Usage Warning** (Page 85):
   - **Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations.** They are not designed for frequent use.

2. **Resetting Emergency Stop** (Page 18):
   - When the robot enters an Emergency stop state, the indicator shows **blinking blue**
   - This means: "The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)"

3. **Diagnosing Emergency/Protective Stop Issues** (Page 99):
   - When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by:
     - Signing in to the robot interface
     - Viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**

4. **Maintenance Check** (Page 178):
   - In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, check if:
     - Front scanner cover is Clean
     - Back scanner cover is Clean

5. **Protective Stop Signal** (Page 96):
   - The robot uses pins 2 in interfaces A and B of the Auxiliary safety functions for the Safeguarded stop function
   - If both pins deliver 24 V to the robot, it can operate
   - If either or both pins deliver 0 V, the robot enters Protective stop

### Summary:
The manual emphasizes that emergency stops should only be used in actual emergencies. To reset an emergency stop, the robot displays a blinking blue light indicating it's waiting for user action. The safety system diagnostics can be accessed through the robot interface to identify which specific component triggered the stop. Regular maintenance should include checking that scanner covers are clean.

For more detailed information, you can view the manual directly at:

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I need to find the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) for the MiR250 robot and then search its documentation for information about emergency stops.

Let me start by finding the MiR250 asset in the graph.









I need to use exact matching for idShort. Let me first get the templates index and then try a different approach to find the MiR250 asset.









Now let me try to find the MiR250 asset by listing all assets and looking for one that might be the MiR250. I'll use a different approach - list all AAS shells first.









I found the MiR250 assets. There are:
- `urn:aas:mir250:001` (MiR250_001)
- `urn:aas:mir250:002` (MiR250_002)
- `urn:aas:mir250:type` (MiR250_Type)

The type shell likely contains the documentation. Let me check the submodels of the type shell to find documentation or technical data submodels that might contain PDF manuals.









I found several submodels for the MiR250 type. The "HandoverDocumentation" submodel is likely to contain PDF manuals. Let me also check if there are any File elements in these submodels that might be PDF documents.









I found a PDF file `/aasx/files/MiR250.pdf` in the HandoverDocumentation submodel. Now I can search this document for information about emergency stops.









I found comprehensive information about emergency stops in the MiR250 manual. Let me summarize the key points from the documentation:

## Emergency Stop Information from MiR250 Manual

According to the MiR250 operator manual (found in the HandoverDocumentation submodel), here's what it says about emergency stops:

### **Basic Emergency Stop Operation** (Page 84)
- The robot enters **Emergency stop** when an Emergency stop button is pressed physically
- When pressed, internal safety contactors are switched so the robot's top application and all moving parts do not receive power (you can hear audible clicks)
- The robot's status lights turn red when in Emergency stop
- The robot cannot be moved or sent on missions until brought out of Emergency stop

### **How to Resume** (Page 84)
1. Release the Emergency stop button
2. Press the **Resume button** (which begins flashing blue after releasing the E-stop)
3. The robot immediately resumes an operating state after pressing the flashing Resume button

### **Emergency Stop Circuit** (Pages 86, 94)
- The Emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module
- Any number of Emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit
- When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop
- The interface uses two output pins to provide a 24 V signal and two input pins to bring the robot into Emergency stop
- The 24 V signal passes through all Emergency stop buttons and continues to the two input pins
- When both input pins receive 24 V, the robot can operate
- Pressing E-stop buttons breaks the circuit so both inputs receive 0 V, triggering Emergency stop
- If the circuit or E-stop button is installed incorrectly (input signals not the same), the robot enters **Protective stop** until fixed

### **External Emergency Stop Interface** (Page 205)
The manual describes two auxiliary safety interfaces (A and B) with:
- **Shared emergency stop in** pins (inactive = robot goes into emergency stop)
- **Shared emergency stop out** pins (inactive when robot is in emergency stop)
- Support for connecting additional emergency stop buttons through the electrical interface

### **Key Points**
- MiR250 has **one built-in Emergency stop button** plus the option to connect **additional E-stop buttons** through the electrical interface
- The system uses a **24 V safety circuit** that must remain intact for normal operation
- Breaking the circuit (by pressing any E-stop button) immediately triggers Emergency stop

You can view the relevant pages in the manual:
- [Page 84 - Emergency stop description](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=84)
- [Page 86 - Emergency stop circuit](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)
- [Page 94 - Emergency stop circuit details](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=94)
- [Page 205 - Safety interface pinout](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)
